skill.name: prisma-expert
skill.description: Prisma ORM guidance for schema design, migrations, query optimization, relation modeling, and database operations.
skill.summary: Diagnose and improve Prisma schemas, migrations, queries, relations, and connection handling.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, filesystem, environment, and heuristic patterns, but most are false positives caused by Markdown fences, official Prisma documentation links, and illustrative code samples. The confirmed concern is operational: the skill recommends Prisma migration, reset, resolve, and database inspection commands that can change databases if executed without explicit user review.

medium_findings.title: Database Migration Commands Require Review
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends Prisma migration commands, including development resets and production deploy or resolve actions. These are legitimate Prisma operations, but they can modify schema state or destroy development data if run automatically or against the wrong database.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly discuss migration reset, deploy, resolve, production testing, and rollback planning. The risk is operational misuse rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Database Connection Inspection Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill includes a psql command for checking active PostgreSQL connections. This is useful diagnostics, but it can access a real database depending on the active environment and credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is concrete and database-facing, but it performs a read-only count query and does not show credential collection or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fences Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis classified many backticks and fenced examples as Ruby or shell execution. The file is Markdown guidance, not executable Ruby code, so these locations are false positives except where they document commands a user may choose to run.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is inside Markdown code fences in SKILL.md. No executable script file, dynamic evaluation, or command injection path is present.

low_findings.title: Official Documentation Links Only
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Prisma documentation pages. I did not find evidence of data exfiltration endpoints, tracking webhooks, or commands that send local data to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed URLs all use the prisma.io documentation domain. The surrounding text presents them as reference resources only.

low_findings.title: Illustrative Environment Variable Usage
low_findings.description: The skill shows NODE_ENV usage and an example DATABASE_URL with placeholder credentials. It does not instruct the agent to read a .env file or expose connection strings, but database URL examples should still be treated carefully.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable references appear in TypeScript and env examples for connection management. No evidence found of secret harvesting or network transmission.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptography and reconnaissance patterns, but the inspected context is Prisma schema, relation, migration, query, and checklist guidance. I did not find cryptographic operations, malicious reconnaissance, or prompt injection text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanner appears to match common substrings in domain-specific prose. Manual review found no code implementing weak hashes, scanning networks, or overriding evaluator instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Prisma Reset Mentioned
dangerous_patterns.description: The guidance mentions resetting a development database with Prisma migrate reset. This can destroy local data and should require explicit user confirmation before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line directly names the reset operation. It is scoped to development in the text, which lowers maliciousness but not operational impact.

dangerous_patterns.title: Production Migration Commands Mentioned
dangerous_patterns.description: The guidance includes production migration deployment and resolve commands. These are valid Prisma workflows, but they can alter production database migration state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly production-related and database-changing. The surrounding text also warns against migrate dev in production, which indicates legitimate intent.

content.value_statement: Prisma projects often fail because schemas, relations, migrations, and queries drift from database reality. This skill gives focused review steps, safer migration guidance, and practical optimization patterns for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Diagnoses Prisma schema, relation, migration, query, connection, and transaction issues.
Provides Prisma CLI validation and migration workflow guidance.
Explains relation modeling, indexes, mapping, and schema organization patterns.
Reviews Prisma Client query patterns for N+1 queries and over-fetching.
Suggests connection lifecycle and pooling approaches for serverless and production environments.
Offers a Prisma-focused code review checklist for schema quality, performance, and migration safety.
limitations: Does not replace a database backup, staging test, or production change approval process.
Raw SQL tuning and database server configuration are outside its main scope.
Examples may need adaptation for the project database provider and deployment environment.
Migration commands can be risky if run against the wrong database.

use_cases.title: Fix Schema Relation Errors
use_cases.description: Identify missing relation fields, incorrect references, and index gaps in a Prisma schema.

use_cases.title: Plan Safer Migrations
use_cases.description: Review migration status, drift, rollback planning, and production deployment steps before database changes.

use_cases.title: Improve Query Performance
use_cases.description: Find N+1 queries, excessive includes, missing selects, and index opportunities in Prisma Client usage.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Prisma Schema
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Prisma schema for relation mistakes, missing indexes, and field type issues. Explain the safest fixes first.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose Migration Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me diagnose this Prisma migration failure. Review the error, migration history, and schema change, then suggest a safe recovery path.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Prisma Queries
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these Prisma Client queries for N+1 patterns, over-fetching, missing pagination, and index needs. Recommend concrete improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Production Database Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit our Prisma production migration workflow. Check deployment safety, rollback planning, connection handling, and commands that need human approval.

output_examples.input: A schema has User, Post, and Profile models with unclear relation names and slow author lookups.
output_examples.output: The response identifies relation ambiguity, recommends explicit relation names, adds indexes for author lookups, and explains validation steps.

output_examples.input: A migration failed after a column rename in a shared development database.
output_examples.output: The response separates development and production recovery, checks migration status, explains drift risk, and recommends review before resolve commands.

output_examples.input: A route loads users and then loads posts for each user in a loop.
output_examples.output: The response flags the N+1 pattern, recommends relation loading or selective nested fields, and asks for pagination requirements.

best_practices: Validate schemas and migrations in a disposable environment before applying changes to shared databases.
Use explicit relation definitions, useful indexes, and selective query fields for predictable Prisma behavior.
Require user confirmation before running reset, deploy, resolve, or direct database commands.
anti_patterns: Do not run Prisma reset or migration commands against production without backup and approval.
Do not include large relation trees when a small selected field set is enough.
Do not expose DATABASE_URL values or paste real credentials into prompts.

faq.question: Can this skill design a Prisma schema from requirements?
faq.answer: Yes. It can propose models, relations, indexes, and naming conventions based on the requirements you provide.

faq.question: Can it fix failed migrations automatically?
faq.answer: It can recommend recovery steps, but migration repair should be reviewed and approved before any command runs.

faq.question: Does it support PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQLite?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance covers Prisma patterns across those providers, with provider-specific checks when needed.

faq.question: Can it tune raw SQL queries?
faq.answer: Only at a basic Prisma integration level. Deep raw SQL tuning should use a database-specific expert.

faq.question: Is it safe for production databases?
faq.answer: It is safe as guidance, but production commands require backups, staging validation, and explicit human approval.

faq.question: What information should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide the Prisma schema, relevant queries, migration error text, provider, deployment target, and any constraints.
